title: VMware Aria Operations Unauthenticated Command Injection Enables Full vSphere Infrastructure Takeover via Support-Assisted Migration Endpoint — CISA KEV Added March 3, 2026 with March 24 Federal Deadline (CVE-2026-22719, CVSS 8.1)

sources.name: CISA Adds Two Known Exploited Vulnerabilities to Catalog (March 3, 2026)
The Hacker News - CISA Adds Actively Exploited VMware Aria Operations Flaw to KEV Catalog
BleepingComputer - CISA Flags VMware Aria Operations RCE Flaw as Exploited in Attacks
SecurityWeek - VMware Aria Operations Vulnerability Exploited in the Wild
Dark Reading - VMware Aria Operations Bug Exploited, Cloud Resources at Risk
RedPacket Security - CVE Alert: CVE-2026-22719 VMware Aria Operations
Prism News - CISA Adds VMware Aria Operations Command-Injection Flaw to KEV

timeline.event: Broadcom publishes VMSA-2026-0001 disclosing CVE-2026-22719 and releasing patches for Aria Operations 8.18.6 and Cloud Foundation 9.0.2.0
Temporary shell-script workaround aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh made available in KB430349 for organizations unable to patch immediately
CISA adds CVE-2026-22719 to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, confirming active exploitation in the wild
CISA mandatory remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies

simple_summary.what_happened: A serious security flaw was found in VMware Aria Operations, the platform that many large organizations use to monitor and manage their entire virtual infrastructure — including servers, virtual machines, and cloud environments. The vulnerability is in the support-assisted product migration feature, where the software fails to properly check user-supplied input, allowing attackers to sneak malicious commands into the system. Broadcom released a patch on February 24, 2026, but attackers were already exploiting it by March 3 when CISA added it to their list of actively exploited vulnerabilities.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running VMware Aria Operations versions 8.18.0 through 8.18.5, or VMware Cloud Foundation versions 9.0 through 9.0.1.x. This includes large enterprises, government agencies, financial institutions, and service providers that rely on VMware for their virtualized data center operations. Federal agencies have a mandatory deadline of March 24, 2026 to patch.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies an internet-accessible or network-reachable VMware Aria Operations management interface (typically on port 443 or 8443). Step 2: Attacker sends a specially crafted HTTP request to the support-assisted migration API endpoint — no username or password required. Step 3: The request contains shell metacharacters (semicolons, pipes, backticks) embedded in migration parameters that bypass input validation. Step 4: The Aria Operations service executes the attacker's injected commands with service-level privileges, granting initial foothold on the management appliance. Step 5: Attacker harvests stored credentials, authentication tokens, and vCenter API keys from the compromised Aria Operations server. Step 6: Using stolen credentials, attacker pivots to vCenter and the entire virtualized infrastructure — gaining the ability to deploy backdoors, steal data, and launch ransomware across all managed virtual machines.
what_you_should_do: Immediately upgrade VMware Aria Operations to version 8.18.6 or later, and VMware Cloud Foundation to version 9.0.2.0 or later per VMSA-2026-0001
If immediate patching is not possible, download and apply the temporary workaround script aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh from KB430349 on each Aria Operations appliance node
Restrict network access to the Aria Operations management interface using firewall rules — only allow connections from trusted administrative networks and VPN clients
Review Aria Operations logs for unusual process spawning, unexpected commands, or shell metacharacter patterns in migration logs going back to at least February 24, 2026
If any signs of compromise are found, rotate all credentials stored in or accessible from the Aria Operations instance, including vCenter service accounts and API tokens
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: VMware Aria Operations is not a peripheral system — it has administrative-level access and visibility into every virtual machine, server, network, and workload in the environment it monitors. Compromising Aria Operations is effectively the same as compromising your entire data center. Attackers who gain access can see every system, extract credentials, disable monitoring (making other attacks invisible), and move laterally across the entire infrastructure. The CISA KEV listing confirms this is not a theoretical risk: real attackers are actively exploiting this vulnerability right now, and federal agencies have been given only 21 days to patch.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the unauthenticated command injection flaw in VMware Aria Operations' support-assisted migration API endpoint exposed over the network
Injected shell metacharacters cause arbitrary OS commands to execute through the Aria Operations service process, using shell interpreters like bash or sh
After gaining code execution on the Aria Operations appliance, attackers harvest vCenter credentials, API tokens, and service account passwords stored in configuration files
Stolen vCenter credentials and API tokens allow attackers to move laterally across the entire managed virtual infrastructure without additional exploitation
Compromising the monitoring platform allows attackers to suppress alerts, disable logging, and hide malicious activity from security teams

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on abnormal process spawning from the Aria Operations migration service, unexpected command execution patterns, and suspicious network connections originating from the Aria Operations appliance. Given the unauthenticated nature, monitor for requests to migration API endpoints from unexpected source IPs.

log_sources.description: Monitor /var/log/ on the Aria Operations appliance for unexpected process spawning, shell command execution during migration activities, and error messages indicating injection attempts
Review logs for access to Aria Operations management ports (443, 8443) from unexpected source IPs, particularly external or non-administrative subnets
Monitor for unexpected API calls, new admin account creation, or bulk VM operations originating from service accounts associated with Aria Operations
If an EDR agent is deployed on the Aria Operations appliance, review for process trees showing web service processes spawning shell interpreters (bash, sh, python)

what_to_look_for.indicator: Shell metacharacters in HTTP request parameters to migration endpoints
what_to_look_for.description: Requests containing semicolons, pipe characters, backticks, or dollar signs in migration-related API parameters may indicate injection attempts
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.request.uri contains ';' OR '|' OR '`' AND uri contains 'migration'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected child processes from Aria Operations service
what_to_look_for.description: The Aria Operations service process spawning shell interpreters (bash, sh) or system utilities (wget, curl, nc) outside of normal maintenance windows indicates exploitation

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound connections from Aria Operations appliance to external IPs
what_to_look_for.description: New outbound connections from the management appliance to external/non-trusted IP addresses, particularly on high-numbered ports, suggest a reverse shell or C2 beacon
what_to_look_for.log_field: src.ip=<aria_ops_ip> AND dst.ip NOT IN <trusted_ranges> AND dst.port NOT IN [443,80,25,53]

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unauthenticated requests to migration API endpoints
what_to_look_for.description: HTTP requests to migration-related API paths from IPs that have not previously authenticated to the management interface
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.request.uri contains '/support' OR 'migration' AND http.response.status=200 AND auth.user IS NULL

immediate_actions.action: Patch Aria Operations to 8.18.6 or apply workaround
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade VMware Aria Operations to version 8.18.6 (for 8.x deployments) or VMware Cloud Foundation Operations to 9.0.2.0 (for 9.x). If immediate patching is not possible, apply the temporary workaround script aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh available in KB430349 as root on each appliance node.
immediate_actions.command:
# Check current version
curl -sk https://<aria-ops-host>/suite-api/api/version

# Apply workaround if patching is delayed
bash aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh

immediate_actions.action: Restrict network access to Aria Operations management interfaces
immediate_actions.description: Immediately apply firewall rules to limit access to the Aria Operations web interface (port 443/8443) to only authorized administrative IP ranges. Remove any internet-facing exposure of the management console.
immediate_actions.command:
# Example iptables rule to restrict access
iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -s <trusted_admin_subnet> -j ACCEPT
iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP

immediate_actions.action: Audit logs for signs of exploitation since February 24
immediate_actions.description: Review Aria Operations application logs, web server access logs, and system audit logs from February 24, 2026 onwards for shell metacharacter patterns, unexpected process execution, and unauthorized access to migration endpoints.

immediate_actions.action: Rotate credentials if exploitation is suspected
immediate_actions.description: If log review reveals any suspicious activity, immediately rotate all credentials accessible from Aria Operations: vCenter service accounts, cloud platform API keys, LDAP/AD service accounts, and any credentials stored in the Aria Operations configuration.
